excerpt: BIP-125 opt-in RBF lets a transaction signal its replaceability via the sequence field. Full RBF treats every transaction as implicitly replaceable, removing the zero-conf-as-payment fiction at the cost of breaking some legacy assumptions.
body:
Source excerpt:
BIP-125 opt-in RBF lets a transaction signal its replaceability via the sequence field. Full RBF treats every transaction as implicitly replaceable, removing the zero-conf-as-payment fiction at the cost of breaking some legacy assumptions.

---

Cited by leaf "The mempool, fee estimation, and RBF":
The *mempool* is a node's local cache of unconfirmed transactions it has received and is willing to relay. It is *per-node*, not global — every node's mempool is slightly different, depending on which transactions reached it, in what order, and which policy filters it runs. The mempool is consulted by miners when they assemble candidate blocks, and by wallets when they estimate fees. It is not part of consensus [1].

The distinction between *consensus rules* and *policy* matters here. Consensus rules are the laws every full node enforces: a block is valid or it isn't. Policy is the local convention for which transactions a node is *willing to relay and mine* — non-standard transactions might be perfectly valid in a block, but Core nodes refuse to gossip them onto the network or include them in templates. The mempool is the place where this distinction lives. A non-standard transaction that arrives in a mined block is accepted; the same transaction arriving via P2P gossip is dropped silently [2].

*Replace-by-fee* (RBF) is the mempool policy that allows a transaction to be replaced by a newer transaction that conflicts with it (spends at least one of the same inputs) and pays a higher fee. BIP-125 specifies the original opt-in form: a transaction signals its replaceability by setting a sequence number below `0xfffffffe`. Full RBF, which makes every transaction implicitly replaceable, is a more recent and contested policy change [2]. RBF is what allows users to bump a stuck transaction's fee instead of waiting hours for it to confirm or expire.

*Child-pays-for-parent* (CPFP) is the complementary mechanism: a recipient who finds an incoming transaction stuck can spend its output in a new high-fee transaction, and miners — who select transactions by *package* fee rate, not individual fee rate — will include the parent to claim the child's fee. This lets a recipient unstick a transaction the sender forgot about.

Fee estimation is empirical. Bitcoin Core's `estimatesmartfee` walks the recent confirmation history (how full were recent blocks, how quickly did transactions in fee bucket X confirm) to estimate what fee rate clears within N blocks. Better estimators exist as third-party services, but the Core estimator is conservative-by-design — it errs toward overpaying because the cost of being stuck is much worse than the cost of overpaying by a few sats per vbyte.

---

Cited by leaf "Fee estimation and getting a transaction confirmed":
The Bitcoin fee market is an auction for the next ten minutes of block space. Each block holds roughly 1 MB of transactions weighted by witness; miners include transactions in descending order of fee per virtual byte (sat/vB) until the block is full [1]. If you bid above what the marginal included transaction in the next block paid, you will be in that block. If you bid below, your transaction will sit in mempools at the same priority as everyone else's underbids and confirm whenever fee demand drops to your level — could be the next block, could be next week.

What your wallet's fee picker is reading: most wallets pull from a fee-estimation service (mempool.space, your own node's `estimatesmartfee` RPC, or a vendor's heuristic) that publishes the lowest sat/vB seen included in the last N blocks at various confirmation targets. "Fast" usually means targeting the next block; "medium" means within the next 3–6 blocks; "economy" means within the day. These are estimates of a future auction, not guarantees — fees can spike between when you sign and when you broadcast [2].

What to do when an under-fee transaction is stuck: replace-by-fee (RBF) lets you re-broadcast the same transaction with a higher fee, replacing the original in mempools that respect BIP125 — almost all of them do. Child-pays-for-parent (CPFP) lets you spend an output of the stuck transaction in a new transaction that bundles a high-enough fee to cover both, incentivizing miners to mine the parent in order to mine the child [3]. RBF requires you to have signaled it on the original (most modern wallets do by default); CPFP requires you to have a spendable output of the stuck transaction (often the change). If neither is available, the transaction will eventually drop out of mempools (typically after two weeks of non-confirmation) and the inputs become spendable in a new transaction.
